This can be put as an if then: if a written thing that talks about each thing is true for all things, then that written thing can be shown using the simple steps of the old simple system. The if then goes from meaning to writing, and that is the hard part. The other way, the easy part, says that if we have a written showing of a written thing, then that written thing is true for all things. With the first if then in hand, we can show that a written thing is true by only looking at its meaning.

Think about a written thing that says if each thing has the thing we call the first thing then some thing has the thing we call the first thing. The written thing is made of a first part that talks about each thing having the thing we call the first thing and a second part that talks about some thing having the thing we call the first thing.

To show that this written thing is true for all things, we look at any group that has at least one thing and any way we think about what the thing we call the first thing means for those things. Imagine the first part is true: each thing in the group has the thing we call the first thing. Because the group must have at least one thing, there is at least one thing in the group. That thing has the thing we call the first thing, by the idea we started with. So the second part, that some thing has the thing we call the first thing, is also true. The first part so makes the second part true for every group that has at least one thing, no matter how we think about the thing we call the first thing, so the written thing is true for all things.

Using the idea that meaning and writing fit, and using the step that says if we know the first part and we know that the first part leads to the second part, we can say the written thing can be shown using the simple steps. This shows how the move from talking about each thing to talking about some thing works. In the old way people thought, this move had a different name, but in our system it is allowed because we always keep the idea that a group has at least one thing.

There is no other way that a big car that carries things can take. The lower river road was washed away in a big water rise six years ago and was never made again, and the forest road on the north side has a door and is hard and can not be used by big cars that carry things in any case. The pass is the only way, which means the fact that the pass is shut means things do not move, and that is clear. If the pass shuts because of snow, the night big car thing between the towns stops, full stop. Drivers do not try the run, the company does not set times to take things for the same night, and anything that must be fast is kept at the start place. People in the low land know this and plan for it in the winter months, watching the mountain sky talk the way sea people watch big water rise talk. Last night, though, the things came at the right time — all 12 boxes from the place where things are put, brought before 6 in the morning as the usual plan says. If the road had been shut, the things could not have come through. The things came through. That means the pass was not shut. That is the whole of the thought. ### `misc-conditionals-cond-probabilistic-everyday-child-rearing-0021-reasoning`

**Question:** probabilistic conditional, modus_ponens — child_rearing

**Expected answer (textbook):** There is a kind of change that many people see that often works in how young people who read move through the steps of books for kids, and it is good to see it not as a tall thing that kids climb at set ages but as a step by step change that comes from the more they have read as well as how old they are.  

The kid who has been reading by them for two or more years — reading by choice, reading for fun, reading long enough times to really finish books and come back for more — makes a strong want for story that the first short books stop making happy.  

First short books are made to help new readers who read by them: short parts, few people, easy to guess story lines, things that end fast.  

For a kid with two years of reading by them behind them, those parts stop being help and start feeling slow. The story line is too easy, the parts too short, the end too sure too fast.  

What they want — what they are ready for — is a story line that needs them to keep things in mind over many parts, people who have inside thoughts that make the story more hard, problems that need a real path to end.  

That is the place that books for older kids fill.  

Our boy has been reading by himself since six, taking books and finishing them and asking for the next one, and he is now eight. Two whole years of easy reading by himself for fun.  

The same idea works for him. He will probably have already grown out of the first short books now, even if we keep getting them, and he will probably like more the kind of story and people that books for older kids with doing or make believe give.  

We should change our book place choices in that way.

### `misc-conditionals-cond-probabilistic-everyday-games-and-sport-0054-reasoning`

**Question:** probabilistic conditional, hypothetical_syllogism — games_and_sport

**Expected answer (textbook):** Teaching about the game has come together on how hard you hold the stick as one of the most simple and often wrong parts of playing for fun, and the two step why and what happens line that goes from hold very hard to a big hit that makes a round bad is good to follow clear because both parts in it are real and made from how the body works.  

The first part is about how hard you hold and how the swing works.  

When a player presses the stick with a lot of force — more than a light hold, about a third of the most they can — the feeling of how hard you hold goes up the lower arm into the parts that pull and push the part where two bones meet that moves the hand.  

The parts at the end of the lower arm, instead of bending in a normal way during the back swing and making straight when the stick hits as the swing needs, become hard.  

The stick goes back with less bend at that part than the shape of the swing needs.  

What happens when the bend in the back swing is smaller is a way the stick comes down that is more up and down than best: the stick reaches the ball on a down path that is more up and down than flat, hitting the ball from above instead of through it.  

That is the first part.  

The second part is about the very up and down way the stick comes down and what the hit does.  

A stick that comes down very up and down into the ball will hit the ground before the ball on any swing where the path is not just right, making a deep hole in the ground behind the ball and making the hit go through that hole instead of at the low point of the path where the ball sits.  

What happens is less distance, wrong direction, and a big sound of a big hit that every person who plays for fun knows with a sad feeling.  

Put the two parts together: hold very hard and you will probably make a big hit.  

Both if parts are real and the line is good.

**Expected answer (textbook):** Think about three things that always go without big changes.  

The first thing is the thing that takes a number that goes in and gives the number times itself. It goes without big changes at every point on the line of true numbers. It works everywhere; for every line of numbers that gets closer to a given point, the new line of numbers also gets closer to the number times itself of that point; and it turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that also stay not too big.  

The second thing is the thing that takes a number that goes in and gives a number that gets bigger fast when the number that goes in gets bigger. It goes without big changes at every point. It works everywhere; it keeps the idea that when a line of numbers gets closer to a point, the new line also gets closer; and it turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that also stay not too big.  

The third thing is the thing that takes a number that goes in and gives a number that goes up and down in the same way. It goes without big changes at every point. It works everywhere; it keeps the idea that when a line of numbers gets closer to a point, the new line also gets closer; and it itself stays not too big, so it also turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that stay not too big.  

From these cases we can make a thought: a thing that changes a number into another number is without big changes at a point if and only if it works there, it keeps the idea that when a line of numbers gets closer to that point the new line also gets closer, and it turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that stay not too big in every small area around the point.  

The first two thoughts are the well known way of saying without big changes with lines of numbers, which is the same as the usual small number thought and everyone says it is right.  

The third thought – that the thing turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that stay not too big in small areas around the point – is true for all three case things and might look like a different needed thought. Yet it is not different. It comes from the line thought. If a thing keeps the idea that every line of numbers that gets closer to a point also gets closer after the thing, then it stays not too big in every small area around that point, and so it turns sets of numbers that stay not too big in that area into sets of numbers that stay not too big. The third thought adds no new thought that is already in the second. The case things were all good things that showed many nice things beyond just being without big changes, and the thought has taken one such nice thing – staying not too big – and raised it up as a different thought, when in truth it follows from the line thought as a clear end. All the tiny parts of the second thing are the same kind and there are no other things together. It is hard, dark, metal, it is made of tiny parts that all have the number 26, and it also has its own set of things it does.  

All the tiny parts of the third thing are the same kind and there are no other things together. It is an air like thing with no color and no smell that is needed for breathing, it is made of tiny parts that all have the number 8.  

Each thing is made of tiny parts of one kind, has its own set of things it does, and is all the same way all over when we see it in the usual way.  

We could write a thought: a thing is a single part thing if it is made of tiny parts that all have the same number, it has its own set of things it does, and it is all the same way all over when we see it in the usual way.  

Now think about the thing with the number 6. Any person who study things would call it a single part thing. Yet the thing does not have just one set of things it does.  

The hard clear form of the thing with number 6 – tiny parts put together so each tiny part is put together with four others in a shape that goes in three ways – is the hardest thing we know, clear, very bright, and it does not let force move, and it needs a very high hot point where it turns to a hot soft form.  

The soft dark form of the thing with number 6 – tiny parts put together in flat sheets that look like many little flat pieces – is one of the softest hard things we know, not clear, dark, and so good at letting force move that we use it in pieces that help move force.  

The two forms have different ways they are heavy for their size, different ways the tiny parts are put together, different ways they look with light, and different ways they let force move.  

When we look at them with our eyes, they are as different as two things can be.  

In the usual way we see it, the thing with number 6 is not all the same way all over; it can be the hard clear form or it can be the soft dark form, and both are okay to call it the thing with number 6.  

The second and third parts of the thought are not true.  

The earlier cases were things that happen to be only one usual form – one form of the thing with number 79, one form of the thing with number 26, one form of the thing with number 8 – and the thought took this same as needed.  

The thing with number 6 shows that what makes a thing a single part thing is not the way it looks or acts on a big size but the fact that its tiny parts are the same, and that one single part thing can look very different on how its tiny parts are put together.

### `cxbot-abdcx-abdcx-philosophy-of-science-classification-2003-reasoning`

**Question:** counterexample to: A system of classification C is scientifically adequate if and only if (i) C gro

**Expected answer (textbook):** The best ways that people have put things into groups — a set of many small parts that are put in order by the way their inside parts are built, a way that a well known old person put living things into groups by the way their bodies look, a way that people put rocks into groups by the way their tiny parts are built — all have a same good point.  

Each one puts things together by parts about them that you cannot just see on the outside but that are part of the thing itself and that make other parts about the thing that people who study care about.  

The first set puts many small parts together by parts that come from the way their inside parts are built; the second way puts living things together by the way their bodies look, which shows they share a same family; the rock way uses the way the tiny parts are built, which decides how hard they are, how they break, and how they let light go through.  

From these big case we can try to make a way to put things into groups that is good for study only when it puts together things that share big important parts, when the groups stay the same when we see new things, and when the whole way fits with the best ideas we have.  

But this way, even though it works for the big case, also lets in ways to put things together that are not good for study.  

Think about a way to put stars into groups by how bright they look from our world — how much light we see.  

Stars that look about the same bright share a part that matters when we look at them; new stars we find can be given a bright look number without changing the groups; and this way of grouping fits well with the ideas we have about space, because the bright look number is a clear thing we can see.  

All three parts of the way are true.  

But this way of grouping does not tell us much about the stars, because it puts together stars that are really very different — a small close star that is not bright and a huge far star that is very bright can look the same bright — and it puts together stars that are really the same.  

The part we use to make the groups — how bright they look — is about where we are looking from, not about any real part of the stars.  

The big case worked because it used parts that belong to the things themselves, not because of where we look at them.  

The way says the parts we group by must be important, but it does not say they must be parts that belong to the things themselves and not to the way we see them.

The idea that each part stays inside makes sure no part goes outside the whole.  

The idea that the parts together fill the whole makes sure the whole is completely covered.

But some may see that this look of parts is too hard for many wholes we see in the real world.

But they share some of the same space: blood lines go through feeling part, small blood lines go into the head and the back bone, and you cannot put one of these parts in a place without the other also being there.  

The idea that parts do not share space does not work here, but no one would say that the blood group and the feeling group are not parts of the body.  

The idea comes from the look of cutting a thing into parts that do not share space, but the real way a living thing is made is into work parts that can go through each other free.

### `cxbot-defcx-defcx-metaphysics-time-1702-reasoning`

**Question:** counterexample to: A moment T is in the past if and only if (i) T has occurred — that is, the event

**Expected answer (textbook):** Someone could say that a time is in the past only when it has happened, it is not now, and it is kept in the memory of at least one mind that felt it. That idea has a kind of easy feeling. When we think about things that happened before — a day when we were small and had food, a walk in the morning on a day before, a paper that people signed — we think about things that have happened, that are not happening now, and that are kept in the memory of the people who saw them. Memory looks like the way the past stays alive; without it, someone could think the past would be nothing — just an empty space where something used to be. But we must think about how the earth was made. That thing happened many many years ago, far before any mind was there. Hot rock came together from a cloud of dust and air; the outside got cold; the first hard outside part became hard over many years. These things have happened and are not now. They are, by any good story, in the past. But no mind saw them; no memory keeps them. We guess they happened from the rock story — from what rocks are made of, from the way rock parts change over time, from space rocks — but this guess is not memory. Memory needs a mind that was there at the thing and that keeps a thought of it, and there was no mind like that when the earth was made. The third part of that idea does not work. The idea has joined together the way we most often understand the past — memory — with what makes something past. A time does not need a person who saw it to have happened and to be not now; the past goes far beyond what any mind can touch, and most of what has happened happened without any mind to see it.

### `reasoning-0220182`

**Question:** why saying something over and over does not make it true

**Expected answer (textbook):** Person one: Why do some people think that if they say a thing many times, it becomes true?

Person two: They think that the more they say it, the more it will stay in your head. They hope that if you hear it enough, you will believe it is a real thing.

Person one: But does hearing it many times change if the thing is real or not?

Person two: No. The truth of a thing does not change because of how many times a person says it. A thing is true or it is not true resting on what is real in the world.

Person one: So if I say that the sky is green many times, does it become green?

Person two: No. The sky stays the color it is. Saying it many times only changes what is in your mind, not what is in the world.

Person one: Is that why people try to make us believe things that are not true by saying them over and over?

Person two: Yes. They want to use your mind to make a thing seem real when it is not. They want you to forget to look at what is actually real.

#### Reasoner pipeline

**1. English query (built from instruction + question):**

> why saying something over and over does not make it true

**2. Translator output (English → UGF):**

> why saying something over and over does not make it true

**3. Wrapped in CONTENT_TYPES template (`socratic_dialogue`) — what the Reasoner actually saw:**

> Write a short conversation between two people about the following topic. One person asks questions, the other explains. The conversation should go at least 6 turns. Use plain text only -- no asterisks, no bold.
> 
> Topic: why saying something over and over does not make it true
> 
> Conversation:
> Person 1:

**4. SFT Reasoner UGF response:**

> Person number one: Why do people think that if they say a thing many times, it becomes a true thing?
> 
> Person number two: It is because the mind can get used to hearing the same words. When we hear a thing many times, it starts to feel like it must be right.
> 
> Person number one: But does feeling that something is right make it real?
> 
> Person number two: No. Feeling that something is right is not the same as the thing being true. You can feel that the moon is made of a white food that we eat, but the moon is not made of that food.
> 
> Person number one: So if i say the moon is made of that white food many times, will my mind believe it?
> 
> Person number two: Yes, your mind might start to think it is true because it is easy to remember. But the moon is still made of rock. Saying a thing does not change the world.
> 
> Person number one: Is there a way to know if a thing is true then?
> 
> Person number two: You must look at the world and see if the thing is the same as what is real. You must look for things that show the thing is real. Do not just listen to how many times a thing is said.

**5. Translator output (UGF → English):**

> Barnaby: 'Why do people think that if they say something many times, it becomes a fact?' Barnaby: 'It's because the mind can get used to hearing the same words. When we hear something many times, it starts to feel like it must be true.' Barnaby: 'But does feeling that something is true make it true?' Barnaby: 'No. Feeling that something is true is not the same as being true. You can feel that the moon is made of cheese, but the moon is not made of cheese.' Barnaby: 'So if I say the moon is made of cheese many times, will my mind believe it?' Barnaby: 'Yes, your mind might start to believe it because it's easy to remember. But the moon is still made of rock. Saying something is true doesn't change the world.' Barnaby: 'Is there a way to know if something is true then?' Barnaby: 'You must look at the world and see if the thing is the same as what is true. You must look for evidence. Don't just listen to how often something is said.'

**2. Comparator UGF response:**

### `reasoning-0481042`

**Question:** why being good at being a person is like being good at anything else

**Expected answer (textbook):** Person number one: Why do you say that being a good person is like being good at other things?

Person number two: Because it is all about how you spend your time and how much you do a thing again and again.

Person number one: Do you mean that being kind or being right with others is something you must do many times to get better?

Person number two: Yes. If you want to be good at a game, you must play the game many times. If you want to be a good person, you must make good choices many times.

Person number one: But is it not different? A game has clear ways to win or lose. Being a person is not always so clear.

Person number two: It is true that it is hard to see the way. But you still have to look at what you do and see if it was good or bad. You have to learn from what you did.

Person number one: So you are saying that you make it a way of life to be good, just like you make it a way of life to play a song well?

Person number two: That is the very same thing. You train your mind to choose the right way until it becomes the way you always go.

**Question:** when helping the group costs you but helps everyone

**Expected answer (textbook):** Person number one: I have a question about what to do when doing something good for the whole group makes my own life harder.

Person number one: But if everyone does this, does the whole group become better?

Person number two: Yes. When each person gives a little bit of what they have, the group becomes very strong. The things that the group has can help everyone stay safe and happy.

Person number two: You look at what you lose and what the group gets. If the good that comes to the whole group is much bigger than the bad that comes to you, then it is a good thing to do.

Person number one: What if I am the only one doing it?

Person number two: Then it is very hard. If only one person gives and the others only take, that person will soon have nothing left. For it to work well, many people must do it together.

**Expected answer (textbook):** Person number one: I want to know about the idea that we should do what makes the most good for all people.

### `reasoning-0051444`

**Question:** how a group can agree on rules that are good for everyone

**Expected answer (textbook):** A group of people wants to live or work together in a way that stays good and gives everyone what they need. To do this, they need to make a set of ways to act. These ways tell people what they can do and what they must not do. If everyone follows these ways, the group can stay together without fighting. But making these ways is very hard.

The hard question is how to find the ways to act that are good for every single person in the group in a way that is not wrong. It is easy to make ways that are good for some people but bad for others. It is also hard to know if a way is good for the whole group or if it only helps the people who have the most power. How do we know if we are being good to the person who has the least?

Some people say the best ways are the ones that make many people feel very good. They think if a way helps many people, even if it hurts a few, it is the right way to go. They look at how much good there is and try to make that number as big as it can be. They think this is the best way to take care of the group.

Other people say this is not right. They say that every person has certain things that should never be taken away. They believe that even if a way makes many people feel very good, it is wrong if it hurts one person in a way that is not right. They think we must have ways that keep every single person safe, no matter what. They think we should not use people just to make the group feel very good.

There is also a way of thinking where people try to make ways that they would agree to if they did not know who they would be in the group. Imagine you do not know if you will be the leader or the person with no money. You do not know if you will be strong or have little power. If you had to pick the ways to act before you knew your place, what would you pick? Most people would pick ways that are safe and right for everyone, because they might end up being the person at the bottom. This is a way to find what is good for all.

> how a group can agree on rules that are good for everyone

The question is what method the group should use to pick those ways so that the result feels right to as many people as possible.

Another answer is to choose a leader or a small group of trusted people to decide the ways for the whole group. Those who give this answer often think that a small number of people can think faster and can keep the group from arguing too long. A third answer is to have the whole group vote on each possible way, so that the most popular choices become the ones the group uses. This answer is based on the thought that a vote lets the group see what most people want and that the result will have the support of the majority. A fourth answer is to try a trial period where the group uses a set of ways for a short time, then talks about what worked and what did not, and changes the ways as needed. People who like this answer often feel that trying it out first lets the group see real effects before they lock in any decision.
> 
> Each of these answers comes from a different belief about what makes a decision feel right: the belief that many voices give fairness, the belief that a small trusted few give speed, the belief that the most common choice gives support, and the belief that testing first gives real knowledge. ### `reasoning-0829829`

**Question:** why sometimes a good enough answer is better than the best answer

**Expected answer (textbook):** Imagine you are building a house. You want the house to be the most perfect house in the whole world. You want the walls to be very strong and the floor to be very even and flat. You want every part of the house to be the very best it can be. But to make the house the very best, you must spend all your money and all your time. You might spend ten years making the windows perfect. While you are doing this, you have no place to live. You are cold and you want food because you are only working on the house.

The hard question is this. Is it better to have a house that is good enough to live in right now, or is it better to wait a long time to have a house that is the best?

Some people will say you must wait for the best. They think that if something is not the best, it is not good to do. They believe that if you stop before you reach the top, you have failed. They want everything to be perfect. They think that if you settle for something that is just good, you are being someone who does not want to work or someone who is not strong. They want the highest point of everything.

Other people will say that the good house is better. They say that time and money are things you cannot get back. If you spend all your time trying to make one thing perfect, you cannot do anything else. You might need to build a second house, or you might need to buy food, or you might need to help a friend. They think that being able to finish something and move to the next thing is more important than making one thing the very best. They think that a good answer that works right now can do more for you than a perfect answer that comes too late.

This is a fight between wanting things to be perfect and needing to live in the real world. The world moves fast. If you wait too long to find the best way to do something, the thing you are trying to do might not be needed anymore. The person who wants the best might end up with nothing, while the person who wants good enough might end up with many good things.

### `reasoning-0937599`

**Question:** the difference between a strong reason and a weak reason

**Expected answer (textbook):** When a person looks at why they do one thing instead of another, they may feel that one reason feels strong and another reason feels not strong. The strong reason feels like it will really push what happens in one way, while the not strong reason feels like it may not do much at all. The hard question is how we can tell when a reason is strong and when it is not strong. It is not just a feeling, it is a question that can make us think a lot about how we decide what to do.

One way people answer is to ask if the reason has many facts that line up with what we already see. When a reason comes with many facts that line up with other things we know, many people say that makes the reason strong. They think that the more facts we have, the more we can be sure the reason will work the way we think. Others say that a reason can be strong even if it does not have a lot of facts, if it fits well with the big picture of what we already see. For them, the fit with the whole set of things we know is more important than a count of facts.

Another group of people look at how the reason makes us feel safe or safe from hurt. If a reason seems to keep us from hurt, they call it strong. If a reason seems to lead us toward a bad place, they call it not strong. They may also think about what the reason will let us do later. A reason that lets us reach more good things later can be called strong, while a reason that stops us from getting good things can be called not strong.

Still another set of people think about how many other people have used the same reason and seen it work. They look at the number of times the reason has been used and the number of times it has helped. If many people say it helped them, they say the reason is strong. If only a few people say it helped, they say it is not strong. Each of these answers comes from a different way of looking at the world, and each can make sense in its own way. The hard question stays the same: what makes a reason strong or not strong, and why do we feel one way about it and another way about another? The answer may be together of facts, fit with what we know, feeling of being safe, and the stories we hear from others.

**Expected answer (textbook):** When you want to understand something that is hard to know, you must take it one small step at a time. This way, you do not get lost in the big thing.

The hard question is this. How do we know if the way we are thinking is good or bad? How do we know if we are moving in the right way or if we are just moving in circles? Some people say that the best way is to look for what is true in every small thing. They say you must find things that cannot be taken away. Other people say that we can never be sure about the small things. They say that everything we think might be wrong in some way. These people think it is better to look at how the small things work together to make a whole. They do not care if one small thing is not true in every single way. ### `reasoning-1370024`

**Question:** why the rules should be the same for everyone

**Expected answer (textbook):** Imagine a group of people living together in one big place. This place has many ways of telling people how to act so that everyone stays safe and happy. These ways are what we call the things everyone must do. Now, imagine that one person is very strong or has a lot of money. This person might think that they should not have to do the same things as the person who has little power or has no money. They might think that because they are important, the ways of acting should be different for them.

The hard question is this. Should the ways of acting be the very same for every single person, no matter who they are or what they have? Should the person with much power follow the same ways as the person with no power?

Some people say yes, the ways must be the same for everyone. They say that if the ways are not the same, then the ways are not good. They think that if one person can do something that another person cannot, then the whole group will break into pieces. They believe that for a group to stay together, everyone must be under the same power. If the ways change because of who you are, then the ways are just ways to help the strong and hurt those who have little. This makes people feel that the group is not what people call right and they will stop wanting to be part of it.

Other people might say no, the ways should not be the same for everyone. They might say that some people need more help than others. To show what they mean, if a person is very sick, they might need different ways of being cared for than a person who is strong and well. They might say that if we act with everyone in the very same way, we might actually hurt the people who are already having a hard time. They believe that being right means looking at what each person needs to live a good life, not just making everyone do the same thing.

These two ways of thinking are hard to bring together. One way wants to make sure no one is above the group, while the other way wants to make sure everyone gets what they need. People spend a long time thinking about how to find a way to do both.

**Question:** why someone's character does not decide if their point is right

**Expected answer (textbook):** Imagine a person who is not a good person. This person might lie a lot or be mean to others. Now, imagine this person says something about how the world works. For one thing, they might say that the sun rises in the east every morning. Even though this person is bad, what they said is still true. The sun does rise in the east. The truth of the thing does not change because the person who said it is bad.

The hard question is this. Does the kind of person someone is change whether what they say is true? Many people think that if a person is bad, we should not listen to them. They think that if a person is bad, their words must be bad too. But this is not how truth works. Truth stays the same no matter who says it.

Some people might say that we should only believe good people. They might say that a bad person will try to make us believe things that are not true. They think that if we listen to a bad person, we might get hurt. For these people, the way a person acts is a way to see if they can be trusted. They use the person to decide if the words are good.

Other people might say that we must look only at the words themselves. They think that if a person says something that can be checked, we should check it. If the person says the sun rises in the east, we look at the sun. We do not look at the person. They believe that truth is not the same thing as the person. They think that if we let the way a person acts change how we see truth, we will lose our way. They think we will stop seeing what is true because we are too busy looking at how people act.

> 
> The hard part of this question is that it mixes two different kinds of things. One kind is about a person’s habits, choices and how they treat other people. The other kind is about a claim or a fact that can be checked by looking at the world. It is easy to think that a kind person must be right and a cruel person must be wrong, because we like to trust people who act well. But it is also easy to see that a kind person can still be mistaken, and a cruel person can still say something that matches what we see in the world. The question therefore is: should we judge the idea by the person, or should we judge the idea by its own match to reality?
> 
> One answer that many people give is “yes, the person matters.” They say that if a person is known to lie or cheat, we should be careful about believing what they say, because they have shown they do not care about the truth. This answer comes from the feeling that we need a quick way to decide whom to listen to, and the past behavior of a person is an easy shortcut.
> 
> Another answer that many people give is “no, the person does not decide.” They argue that a claim can be checked by looking at facts, experiments, or other people’s reports, and that the truth of the claim does not change because the speaker is kind or cruel. > 
> A third view tries to hold both sides together. It says that while the person’s way of being should not be the only thing we use, it is still useful as a hint. If a person has a long record of lying, we should be extra careful and look harder for proof before we accept the claim. This view tries to keep the focus on the claim but also uses the person’s past as a guide.
> 
> All of these answers come from the same place: we want to know when we can trust what someone says. The debate shows that the way a person acts and the truth of what they say are two separate tracks, and we have to decide how much the first track should pull on the second.

### `reasoning-1948754`

**Question:** why being good at being a person is like being good at anything else

**Expected answer (textbook):** We see a lot of talk about how a person can try to be a good person. In this talk a person looks at the way they act, the way they feel, and the way they act toward other people. They watch how they change over time and how other people say what they think. This watching and changing feels a lot like the way a person might try to be good at a game or work. The scene is that many people sit and think about why the work of being a good person seems to follow the same steps as the work of being good at any other thing.

The thing that is happening is that a person takes the thing they want, such as “be nice” or “be just”, and then they look at what they do each day. They get the way other people tell them how they did, from themselves, and from the way the world shows a change. They try again, they learn a little, they try again, and they feel a little better when they see that the world seems a bit nicer. All of this looks a lot like the way a person learns to play a game, to draw, or to fix a thing that works. The hard question that comes up is why the work of being a good person should be thought of as the same as the work of being good at any other thing. Is there something special about the feeling part of being a person, or is it just another set of things they do that can be tried, watched, and changed?

One answer that many people give is that being a good person is just a set of things they do that can be seen and tried. They say that being nice, telling truth, and care are things they do that can be done many times like a game move. When a person watches how those things work, they can do the same as them, they can get a feel for when they work, and they can get better with time. This answer rests on the idea that any thing you get good at, whether it is a game move or a caring act, is learned by trying, by the way other people tell them how they did, and by changing what they do.

Another answer that some people share is that being a good person is not only about things they do but also about feelings and other people. They point out that when you care for someone you feel something inside, and that feeling can push you to act in a certain way. Because feelings are part of the way it goes, they think the work of being a good person may need a different kind of care than the work of fixing a thing you use. They say the need to feel safe, to feel loved, or to feel that you belong can make the learning path a bit different, even if the steps of trying and watching are still there.

A third answer comes from those who look at the ideas that big groups of people make. They say that big groups of people make a set of ideas about what it means to be good, and those ideas act like a game with signs and ideas that tell what to do. In that view, being a good person is like playing a game where you try to follow the ideas, get a good feeling, and avoid a bad feeling. Because a game has a clear set of moves and a clear way to see if you do well, they think the work of being good at a person is very much like the work of being good at any other game.

All of these answers try to tell about why the work of being a good person looks a lot like the work of being good at other things. Some say it is because both are sets of things they do we can try and change. Some say it is because feelings add an extra part that makes it a bit special. Some say it is because the ideas we share act like a game. The talk continues as people think about which of these ideas feels the most true and how they might use that thought to keep trying to be a better person, just as they would keep trying to be better at any other thing they care about.
